fact: The first official cricket Test match was played in 1877 between Australia and England at the Melbourne Cricket Ground (MCG). Australia won by 45 runs.
The Cricket World Cup was first held in 1975 in England, won by the West Indies, who defeated Australia in the final at Lord's.
India won the ICC Cricket World Cup in 2011 on home soil, defeating Sri Lanka in the final at Wankhede Stadium, Mumbai.
A standard cricket pitch is 22 yards (20.12 meters) long, and a Test match innings can last up to 5 days.
The Indian Premier League (IPL), founded in 2008, is one of the most popular Twenty20 cricket leagues in the world.
The Marylebone Cricket Club (MCC), formed in 1787 in London, was long regarded as the guardian of cricket's official Laws of the Game.
Twenty20 (T20) cricket was introduced in England in 2003 as a fast-paced, shorter format of the sport.
The 'Ashes' is a storied Test cricket rivalry between England and Australia, named after a satirical mock obituary published in 1882.
The FIFA World Cup was first held in 1930. Uruguay hosted and won the tournament, defeating Argentina 4-2 in the final match in Montevideo.
Brazil has won the FIFA World Cup a record five times: 1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, and 2002.
A standard football (soccer) match consists of two 45-minute halves, for a total of 90 minutes plus stoppage time.
Argentina won the 2022 FIFA World Cup in Qatar, defeating France on penalties after a 3-3 draw, with Lionel Messi lifting the trophy.
The English Premier League was formed in 1992, replacing the old First Division as the top tier of English football.
The UEFA Champions League, originally called the European Cup, was established in 1955 to showcase Europe's top football clubs.
Red and yellow cards were introduced to football at the 1970 FIFA World Cup to help officials communicate decisions clearly to players.
The offside rule is one of football's most debated regulations, designed to prevent attackers from gaining an unfair positional advantage near the opponent's goal.
The Thomas Cup, established in 1948, is the premier international men's team badminton championship. India won its historic first title in 2022 by defeating Indonesia 3-0.
Badminton became an official Olympic sport at the 1992 Barcelona Games, with singles and doubles events for both men and women.
The shuttlecock, or 'birdie', traditionally has 16 goose feathers attached to a cork base and can travel over 300 km/h when smashed by elite players.
China has historically been one of the most dominant nations in Olympic badminton, winning golds across both singles and doubles events.
The Badminton World Federation (BWF), originally founded as the International Badminton Federation in 1934, governs the sport globally.
The Uber Cup, badminton's premier women's team event, was first held in 1956-57 and was won by the United States.
A badminton court is narrower for singles play than for doubles play, one of the few racquet sports with this distinction.
Indonesia and China are traditionally considered among the strongest nations in international badminton history.
Tennis in its modern form was invented in Birmingham, England in 1873 by Major Walter Clopton Wingfield, who originally called it 'Sphairistike'.
Wimbledon, first held in 1877, is the oldest tennis tournament in the world and the only Grand Slam still played on grass.
Tennis has four Grand Slam tournaments each year: the Australian Open, the French Open (Roland Garros), Wimbledon, and the US Open.
A standard tennis match is played as the best of three sets for most events, or best of five sets for men's Grand Slam singles matches.
Tennis returned to the Olympics at the 1988 Seoul Games after being absent from the program for 64 years.
The French Open, held at Roland Garros in Paris, is the only Grand Slam played on clay courts.
A 'love' score in tennis, meaning zero points, is believed to derive from the French word 'l'oeuf' (egg), resembling the shape of a zero.
The Davis Cup, tennis's premier international men's team competition, was first contested in 1900 between the United States and Great Britain.
Basketball was invented in 1891 by Canadian physical education teacher James Naismith in Springfield, Massachusetts, USA, using peach baskets as goals.
The NBA was founded in 1946 as the Basketball Association of America, adopting the name National Basketball Association in 1949 after merging with a rival league.
The FIBA Basketball World Cup was first held in 1950 in Argentina, with the host nation winning the inaugural title.
Basketball made its Olympic debut as a full medal sport at the 1936 Berlin Olympics.
A standard NBA game consists of four 12-minute quarters, while FIBA international games use four 10-minute quarters.
The three-point line was introduced to the NBA in the 1979-80 season, changing offensive strategy across the sport.
The 1992 US Olympic team, nicknamed the 'Dream Team', was the first to feature active NBA professionals at the Olympics.
A basketball hoop is regulated at a height of 10 feet (3.05 meters) from the court surface at all levels of organized play.
Field hockey traces its modern rules to England, where the Hockey Association was formed in 1886 to standardize the sport.
India won 8 Olympic gold medals in field hockey between 1928 and 1980, one of the most dominant runs in Olympic history.
The Men's Hockey World Cup was first held in 1971 in Barcelona, with Pakistan winning the inaugural tournament.
A standard field hockey match is played over two halves, traditionally 35 minutes each at the international level.
Field hockey has been part of the Olympic program for men since 1908 and for women since 1980.
Astroturf and other synthetic surfaces became standard for elite field hockey starting in the 1970s, significantly increasing the pace of the game.
The International Hockey Federation (FIH), founded in 1924, governs field hockey and indoor hockey worldwide.
A 'penalty corner' in field hockey is awarded for infractions near the goal and is one of the sport's key attacking set-pieces.
Volleyball was invented in 1895 by William G. Morgan in Holyoke, Massachusetts, USA, originally under the name 'Mintonette'.
The Federation Internationale de Volleyball (FIVB), founded in 1947, is the sport's global governing body.
Volleyball became an official Olympic sport for both men and women at the 1964 Tokyo Olympics.
A standard indoor volleyball match is played best-of-five sets, with the first four sets played to 25 points.
Beach volleyball, a variant played on sand with two-player teams, joined the Olympic program at the 1996 Atlanta Games.
The rally scoring system, where a point is scored on every serve regardless of which team served, was adopted internationally in 1999.
A volleyball net stands at different regulation heights for men's and women's competitions, reflecting differences in average reach.
Brazil and the former Soviet Union/Russia are historically among the most successful nations in international volleyball competition.
